metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which dead-code artifacts to remove alongside the loop: `_resolve_retry_budget`, `_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET_DEFAULT`, `_compute_backoff_seconds`, the `lock_max_retries` config key (schema, docs, env-var alias), and their associated tests (`test_config_file_wiring.py:127-147`, `test_ev3c_env_renames.py:52-63`).
findings.finding: Deleting the b859 outer retry loop (lines 263-291) leaves `_resolve_retry_budget`, `_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET_DEFAULT`, `_compute_backoff_seconds`, and the `lock_max_retries` config key (schema, docs, env var, tests) as dead code and dead configuration with no removal specified. The plan lists only the loop lines as deleted; implementers will encounter these orphans without guidance.
findings.location: `_advisory_lock.py` lines 73-89 / `_config_schema.py:334` / `docs/config.md:180`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] When removing the b859 retry loop, also retire the `lock_max_retries` config field, its env-var aliases (`REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`, `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET`), the `_resolve_retry_budget()` helper, the `docs/config.md` entry, and the three test files/cases that exist solely to cover this knob (`test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py`, the `lock_max_retries` cases in `test_config_file_wiring.py`, and `test_ev3c_env_renames.py`).
findings.finding: Deleting the b859 retry loop (`acquire_pass_lock` lines 263-291) orphans the `lock_max_retries` config key and both its env-var aliases (`REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`, deprecated `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET`), leaving dead tunable surface in the schema, config loader, docs, and three test files whose sole purpose is to exercise this knob.
findings.location: AC: 'The b859 retry loop … is deleted'; src/rebar/_config_schema.py:334, src/rebar/config.py:448-452, docs/config.md:180, tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py, tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_file_wiring.py:127-147, tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_ev3c_env_renames.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Remove `ReconcilerConfig.lock_max_retries`, `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`/`REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET` env mappings, `_resolve_retry_budget()`, its call-site, the `docs/config.md:180` entry, and the three dependent test files; verify no remaining reference in the non-integration suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a migration branch to `_commit_gitattributes` (or a companion function) that detects and removes `.reconciler-* merge=ours` from an already-committed `.gitattributes`, mirroring the pattern used by `_commit_gitignore` for existing trackers.
findings.finding: `_commit_gitattributes` in `init.py` is create-if-absent only and will not update `.gitattributes` on already-initialized trackers. Removing `.reconciler-* merge=ours` from `_GITATTRIBUTES` will not purge the old line from trackers that already have a committed `.gitattributes`. The plan does not address this migration gap.
findings.location: What section / `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` — `_commit_gitattributes` function (line 410-425)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `else` branch in `_commit_gitattributes` (after line 414's `if` block): if the file already exists and still contains `.reconciler-* merge=ours`, rewrite it without that line and commit the update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `.gitattributes` migration step — analogous to the existing `.gitignore` line-addition migration in `init.py:365-383` — that removes the `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line from an already-committed `.gitattributes` on `rebar init` re-run.
findings.finding: The `_commit_gitattributes` function in `init.py` is create-only: it skips updating the file if `tickets:.gitattributes` already exists (line 414). The plan proposes removing the `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line via 'migration on reconciler startup or via bridge_fsck/init', but no update path for an existing `.gitattributes` is specified or present. Without a migration write path, existing initialized trackers will retain the obsolete `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line indefinitely.
findings.location: What section: 'Remove .reconciler-* from the merge=ours .gitattributes'; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Model after `_commit_gitignore`'s migration arm: read the current `tickets:.gitattributes` content, strip the `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line if present, and commit the updated file. Add this logic to `_commit_gitattributes` (rename/extend) so it runs on every `init_core` call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an update-migration arm to `_commit_gitattributes` (mirroring the `_commit_gitignore` pattern) that reads the existing `.gitattributes`, strips the `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line if present, and commits the cleaned version — so re-init on existing trackers also satisfies AC1.
findings.finding: The plan removes `.reconciler-* merge=ours` from the `_GITATTRIBUTES` constant in `init.py`, which only affects NEW tracker initializations. `_commit_gitattributes` is create-if-absent (guarded by `if _git(tracker, 'show', 'tickets:.gitattributes').returncode != 0`), so existing trackers with the old pattern committed will never receive the update — the migration is silently incomplete for the population it most needs to fix.
findings.location: What section / init.py:410-426 (_commit_gitattributes); AC line 1 ('.reconciler-* no longer appears in the tickets-branch .gitattributes')
findings.suggested_fix: In `_commit_gitattributes`, after the early-return for a missing file, add an update path: read `git show tickets:.gitattributes`, check whether `.reconciler-*` is present; if so, remove that line, write the cleaned content back, `git add .gitattributes`, and commit with message `'chore: remove .reconciler-* merge=ours (superseded by ref-lock C3)'`. Pattern already used by `_commit_gitignore` (init.py:363-383).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and cite the concrete mechanism that replaces the b859 outer retry loop before removing it — verify that 'lease-based self-heal (C3)' exists in the codebase (or name the ticket/PR that delivers it), since no lease or TTL logic is present in `_advisory_lock.py` or `_concurrency.py` today.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the b859 retry loop is 'superseded by lease-based self-heal' (labeled 'C3'), but no lease mechanism exists in the codebase — `_concurrency.py` implements only a 3-attempt drift-based `rebase_retry` with no TTL, lease file, or fencing token. Removing the outer retry loop without an actual replacement leaves `acquire_pass_lock` with just 3 drift attempts, which may reintroduce the original b859 contention regression.
findings.location: What section: 'Remove the b859-8fa1 lock-retry band-aid loop'; AC line 3: 'contention is handled by the ref-lock CAS/lease path instead'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that C3 (the ref-lock / lease-based self-heal mechanism) is already merged and live before deleting the b859 retry loop, or explicitly gate AC3 on C3 landing — the plan currently asserts a replacement capability that does not exist in the codebase.
findings.finding: The plan justifies deleting the b859 retry loop by asserting 'lease-based self-heal (C3) is authoritative,' but no such ref-lock/lease mechanism exists anywhere in the codebase — `_ref_lock.py` is absent from `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/`, the string `refs/reconciler` appears only in prior plan-review cache files (not in source), and `_concurrency.py`/`_advisory_lock.py` implement only a 3-attempt rebase_retry with no lease. Deleting lines 73–89 and 263–291 of `_advisory_lock.py` without C3 in place removes all outer contention handling with no replacement.
findings.location: What section — 'Remove the b859-8fa1 lock-retry band-aid loop … (superseded by lease-based self-heal)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) sequence this plan as a follow-on after the C3 ref-lock ticket is merged and its integration is verified, or (b) if C3 is intentionally deferred, retain the b859 retry loop and scope this plan only to the .gitattributes + migration steps until C3 lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define or cite the concrete codebase location of the 'ref-lock CAS/lease path (C3)' that AC3 states replaces the b859 loop — or, if C3 is not yet implemented, adjust the plan to retain the retry loop until C3 lands.
findings.finding: The plan's central justification — 'the self-healing ref-lock (C3) is authoritative' — references a protocol/mechanism that does not exist in the codebase. 'C3' is never defined in the plan, `_ref_lock.py` is absent from `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/`, `refs/reconciler/*` does not exist, and `_concurrency.py` implements only a 3-attempt drift guard with no lease semantics. The plan instructs deletion of the b859 loop because 'contention is handled by the ref-lock CAS/lease path instead,' but no such path is defined or implemented.
findings.location: Plan body ('What' section) and AC3: 'The b859 retry loop … deleted; contention is handled by the ref-lock CAS/lease path instead.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises the ref-lock CAS/lease contention path end-to-end (e.g., 'an integration test that races two reconciler instances against a real git repo confirms the second instance retries via the lease path and succeeds without the b859 loop').
findings.finding: Removing the b859 retry loop is a cutover to the ref-lock CAS/lease path for contention handling, but no acceptance criterion exercises this new contention path end-to-end against a live git repository; the only proving command required is 'full non-integration suite green,' which by definition bypasses the live ref-lock operations and cannot confirm the new path handles contention correctly.
findings.location: AC: 'The b859 retry loop (_advisory_lock.py:73-89, 263-291) is deleted; contention is handled by the ref-lock CAS/lease path instead.' and 'Full non-integration suite green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) that verifies the ref-lock CAS/lease path correctly handles the contention scenarios previously covered by the b859 retry loop, e.g. via a targeted concurrency test or by referencing an existing C3 test that covers these cases.
findings.finding: AC3 verifies the b859 retry loop is deleted but does NOT verify that the replacement mechanism (ref-lock CAS/lease path) actually handles the contention scenarios the loop was addressing. The description asserts 'contention is handled by the ref-lock CAS/lease path instead' but this is an uncovered deliverable claim — no criterion tests it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 / What section 'superseded by lease-based self-heal'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '[ ] A test covering concurrent reconciler startup / lock contention confirms the ref-lock CAS/lease path resolves the contention without the b859 loop present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling for the migration write path: define whether startup continues (log-and-skip) or aborts on failure, and confirm the migration is idempotent (safe to re-run if files are already absent).
findings.finding: The migration step (removing committed .reconciler-* files on reconciler startup) is a write operation with no specified error handling: the plan does not say what happens if the git rm+commit fails, whether startup is aborted or degraded, or whether the migration is safe to retry after a partial failure.
findings.location: What section — migration bullet: 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init), clean up any .reconciler-pass-lock/.reconciler-phase-gate left committed'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the migration in a try/except; if files are already absent treat it as success; log the outcome (files removed / nothing to do / error) at INFO/WARNING level; do not abort startup on migration failure — leave a warning and continue.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or point to the existing C3 test) that explicitly exercises contention / concurrent lock acquisition via the CAS/lease path after the b859 loop is removed, confirming correct behavior under contention rather than only confirming the deletion.
findings.finding: The deletion of the b859 retry loop is a behavior cutover (contention handling now defaults to the CAS/lease path), but no AC tests that the replacement path correctly handles contention after removal — AC3 only confirms the loop is deleted, not that the new default path is exercised under contention scenarios.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'The b859 retry loop ... is deleted; contention is handled by the ref-lock CAS/lease path instead'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A contention-scenario unit/integration test covers two concurrent callers racing through the CAS/lease path (not the deleted retry loop) and confirms only one wins with correct backoff/error handling.' If C3 already has this coverage, reference it explicitly so reviewers can verify the gap is closed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback policy to AC4's migration step: specify whether a migration write failure should abort startup (hard-fail), warn-and-continue, or be retried, and document this in the migration code or AC.
findings.finding: The migration step (AC4) specifies no fallback for a write failure. The migration removes `.reconciler-pass-lock`/`.reconciler-phase-gate` from the tickets branch via a git rm + commit sequence — the same path that can fail with CAS contention or tickets-branch-missing errors. The plan is silent on what the executor should do if the migration write fails: block startup, warn-and-continue, or skip.
findings.location: AC4: 'Migration removes any pre-existing committed `.reconciler-*` files; regression test asserts no `.reconciler-*` path is ever committed to the tickets branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the test file path in the plan and AC2 from `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` to `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`, and update that file's assertion at line 51 to assert `.reconciler-*` is ABSENT.
findings.finding: The plan directs updating `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`, but that path does not exist. The actual test file is `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`. Following the plan literally would create a new file in the wrong location and leave the existing interface test (which still asserts `.reconciler-* merge=ours` IS present at line 51) unmodified — causing the full non-integration suite to fail on AC2.
findings.location: AC line: '`tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` updated to assert `.reconciler-*` is absent'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` with `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` everywhere in the plan. In that file, change line 51 from `assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" in show.stdout` to `assert ".reconciler-*" not in show.stdout` (or equivalent), and update the module docstring at line 3-9 to remove the `.reconciler-*` reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct all plan references from `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` to the actual path `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`, and ensure line 51 (`assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" in show.stdout`) is updated to assert absence.
findings.finding: The plan names `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` as the test file to update, but this path does not exist. The actual file is `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`.
findings.location: What section / AC bullet 2: 'update `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` with `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` throughout the plan. Flip line 51 from `assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" in show.stdout` to `assert ".reconciler-*" not in show.stdout`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py` to the plan's edit set, specifying whether it should be deleted entirely or updated to reflect the new contention-handling path.
findings.finding: Removing the b859 retry loop from `_advisory_lock.py` leaves `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py` (5 tests directly exercising `acquire_pass_lock`'s retry budget/backoff) as unenumerated consumers that will either fail or need deletion. The plan does not mention this file.
findings.location: What section / AC bullet 3: 'The b859 retry loop (_advisory_lock.py:73-89, 263-291) is deleted'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC2's test file path to `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` and explicitly state that the existing `assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" in show.stdout` assertion must be changed to assert absence.
findings.finding: AC2 cites `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` as the test file to update, but the actual file lives at `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`. The file at the cited path does not exist. Additionally, the existing test (`test_init_commits_gitattributes_and_configures_driver`) currently asserts `assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" in show.stdout` — the plan must negate this assertion — but the AC only says 'updated to assert `.reconciler-*` is absent,' giving no indication that the current positive assertion must be flipped.
findings.location: AC2: 'tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py updated to assert `.reconciler-*` is absent'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: 'Update `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`: change `assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" in show.stdout` to `assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" not in show.stdout` and confirm `.bridge_state/* merge=ours` assertion is retained.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task to record the retirement decision (b859 loop → C3 self-healing ref-lock is authoritative) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or equivalent design doc so future maintainers have durable context.
findings.finding: The plan retires the b859 band-aid and establishes C3 self-healing ref-lock as authoritative without capturing this architectural decision in any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc update — future maintainers will not know why the retry loop was deleted or what now owns contention handling.
findings.location: What section — b859 removal bullet; AC#3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR entry or a comment block at the top of _advisory_lock.py explaining that contention is now handled by the CAS/lease path and that retry loops are intentionally absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly which function owns the committed `.reconciler-*` cleanup migration (e.g., a new `_cleanup_committed_reconciler_files` helper called from `init_core` alongside `_commit_gitattributes`) and enumerate it as a new edit target in the plan.
findings.finding: The migration in AC bullet 4 (clean up pre-existing committed `.reconciler-*` files on startup or via bridge_fsck/init) is new behavior not present in either `bridge_fsck.py` or `init.py` today — it is a CREATION hunk. The plan does not flag this as out-of-scope or specify which function owns it, leaving the edit target ambiguous.
findings.location: What section / AC bullet 4: 'Migration removes any pre-existing committed .reconciler-* files'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the migration trigger ambiguity: commit to exactly one call site (startup, init, or bridge_fsck) and state the positive rationale for that choice, including its error-handling contract (e.g., 'fail-warn-and-continue' vs. 'block the run').
findings.finding: The migration trigger is written as 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init)' — a genuine unresolved fork between two call sites with different error-handling semantics. Startup (fail-closed) versus bridge_fsck/init (best-effort/reporting) behave differently if the migration git-rm+commit fails, and they have different blast-radii (blocking a reconciler run vs. surfacing a notice). The plan body never resolves this choice.
findings.location: What section, third bullet: 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init)'
findings.suggested_fix: Consider binding the migration to `init` (the existing idempotent setup path) since `init.py` already writes the `.gitattributes` and runs create-if-absent logic — migrating old lock files there keeps all tickets-branch setup in one place, is idempotent by nature, and does not risk blocking a reconciler run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test fixture and proving step that seeds a tickets branch with a pre-committed `.reconciler-pass-lock` file, runs the migration, and asserts the file is absent post-migration.
findings.finding: The migration AC's regression test is described only as 'asserts no .reconciler-* path is ever committed to the tickets branch,' but there is no proving command or described test fixture that demonstrates the one-time cleanup *ran* and removed pre-existing committed files, as opposed to merely asserting a clean state in a fresh repo.
findings.location: AC: 'Migration removes any pre-existing committed .reconciler-* files; regression test asserts no .reconciler-* path is ever committed to the tickets branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the b859 loop removal — e.g. a feature flag, a git revert recipe, or a documented expand-contract sequence (C3 shadowing b859 before full removal).
findings.finding: The removal of the b859 retry loop is a behavioral breaking change with no rollback or back-out plan. The plan asserts C3 is 'authoritative' but does not describe what to do if C3 has a latent bug causing lock contention failures after the band-aid is retired.
findings.location: What section / AC3 — b859 loop removal

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_file_wiring.py` and `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_ev3c_env_renames.py` to the plan's edit set, clarifying whether `_resolve_retry_budget` (and the `lock_max_retries` config wire-up it provides) should be preserved, redirected, or removed.
findings.finding: Removing `_resolve_retry_budget` (and the constants it depends on: `_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET_DEFAULT`, `_BACKOFF_BASE_SECONDS`, etc.) from `_advisory_lock.py` will break callers in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_file_wiring.py` (3 direct calls) and `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_ev3c_env_renames.py` (3 direct calls). These files are not enumerated in the plan.
findings.location: What section / AC bullet 3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the 'What' migration description with AC4 by specifying that the migration removes all `.reconciler-*` files (glob), not just the two named examples.
findings.finding: The 'What' section names two specific files (`.reconciler-pass-lock` / `.reconciler-phase-gate`) as candidates for cleanup, while AC4 uses the broader glob `.reconciler-*`. If any third `.reconciler-*` artifact exists (e.g., a phase-specific variant introduced during development), it would be covered by AC4 but not by the migration code implied by the What description, creating an observable gap between the acceptance criterion and the implementation scope.
findings.location: AC4 vs. What section migration step
findings.suggested_fix: Change the What description to: 'clean up any `.reconciler-*` files left committed on the tickets branch (including but not limited to `.reconciler-pass-lock` and `.reconciler-phase-gate`).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit logging requirement for the migration step: at minimum emit an INFO log listing files removed (or 'nothing to migrate') and a WARNING/ERROR if the step fails.
findings.finding: The migration step introduces a new failure point (startup git write) with no mention of observability — no log statement, metric, or trace indicating whether the migration ran, what it found, or whether it succeeded.
findings.location: What section — migration bullet; AC#4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the migration trigger to a single, named mechanism (e.g., 'add a `_migrate_legacy_locks()` call to `bridge_fsck` only') so the executor does not have to choose between startup, bridge_fsck, or init.
findings.finding: The migration trigger for cleaning up committed .reconciler-* files is written as 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init),' leaving a real design fork unresolved for the executor: whether to add startup logic, extend bridge_fsck, or extend init is a non-trivial architectural decision that changes the implementation substantially.
findings.location: What section — migration bullet: 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init)'
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one: 'Migration runs as a new sub-step of `bridge_fsck` (or `init --repair`). Remove the startup-code option entirely.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the migration entry-point fork: specify exactly ONE of reconciler startup, `bridge_fsck`, or `init` as the migration hook, with a rationale and an error-handling policy (e.g. warn-and-continue vs. hard fail).
findings.finding: The migration trigger for cleaning up committed `.reconciler-*` files is specified as 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init)' — an unresolved fork between three distinct entry points. This is a processing-protocol placement gap: an executor must choose where to hook the migration write, but the plan provides no decision rule to make that choice.
findings.location: Plan body ('What' section, third bullet): 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init), clean up any `.reconciler-pass-lock`/`.reconciler-phase-gate` left committed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document a back-out plan for the one-time migration — e.g. note that `git revert` of the migration commit restores the files, or add a dry-run mode with explicit operator confirmation before destructive commit.
findings.finding: The one-time migration (removing committed `.reconciler-*` files from the tickets branch) is described as irreversible but has no back-out plan. The plan does not address what happens if the migration runs incorrectly or removes wrong files.
findings.location: What section — 'One-time migration: on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init), clean up any .reconciler-pass-lock/.reconciler-phase-gate left committed on the tickets branch'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dry-run/preview mode to the migration that lists files to be removed before committing, and note that rollback is `git revert <migration-commit>` on the tickets branch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the one-time migration commit is idempotent and safe under concurrent execution (e.g., skip if no `.reconciler-*` files are present, use a ref-CAS or similar to ensure only one instance commits the removal).
findings.finding: The migration that removes pre-existing committed `.reconciler-*` files from the tickets branch is triggered 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init)' — but the plan does not specify that this migration is atomic or idempotent under concurrent execution. If two reconciler instances start simultaneously, both may attempt to detect, delete, and commit removal of the same lock files, resulting in a conflicting commit or a double-commit race on the tickets branch.
findings.location: What section — migration step: 'on reconciler startup (or via bridge_fsck/init), clean up any .reconciler-pass-lock/.reconciler-phase-gate left committed'
findings.suggested_fix: Gate the migration with a presence check inside an atomic ref-update (the same lease/CAS path already used by C3): read the current ref, verify `.reconciler-*` files exist in the tree, commit their removal only if the ref hasn't moved since the read, and treat a lost-update error as 'already done by another instance'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify (in the plan and in the migration implementation) that the `.reconciler-*` file cleanup is idempotent: re-running it against a tickets branch where the files are already absent must be a no-op, not an error.
findings.finding: The one-time cleanup of committed `.reconciler-pass-lock`/`.reconciler-phase-gate` files is not specified as idempotent/resumable. If the migration commit partially fails (e.g. the detached-worktree CAS hits its budget during a burst of concurrent writers), a subsequent re-run must not double-delete or error out on already-absent files — but the plan neither states the idempotency invariant nor points to the mechanism that provides it.
findings.location: What section — 'One-time migration' bullet; AC line 4 ('Migration removes any pre-existing committed .reconciler-* files')
findings.suggested_fix: Cite `_delete_file_from_tickets_branch`'s existing `if file.exists()` guard as the idempotency mechanism, or require the migration to use that helper directly — and add an AC sub-bullet: 'cleanup is a no-op when `.reconciler-*` files are already absent'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `init.py` line-number citation from `:55-56` to `:56-57` to point at the actual two pattern lines in `_GITATTRIBUTES`.
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/_commands/init.py:55-56` for the `.reconciler-*` gitattributes pattern, but the actual pattern lines are 56-57 (`line 56: .bridge_state/* merge=ours`, `line 57: .reconciler-* merge=ours`). The cited range 55-56 points one line early and includes the end of the preceding comment, not the second pattern line.
findings.location: What section: 'Remove `.reconciler-*` from the `merge=ours` `.gitattributes` written by `src/rebar/_commands/init.py:55-56`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change `src/rebar/_commands/init.py:55-56` to `src/rebar/_commands/init.py:56-57` in the What section.
